[[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Kk]][[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in Obama City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with Wakasa Province, it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref>総社神社 総神社 總神社 (小浜市)</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
=== Deities Enshrined === The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
=== Location === The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center. ----
=== Architecture and Structure === The shrine complex features several key structures:
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear. ----
=== Historical Background === The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
=== Relationship to Miwa Shrine === A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine. ----
=== Cultural Notes === The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
=== Access and Nearby Features === Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal. __NOINDEX__[[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]]

[[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Kk]][[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in Obama City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with Wakasa Province, it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
=== Deities Enshrined === The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
=== Location === The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
=== Architecture and Structure === The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
=== Historical Background === The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
=== Relationship to Miwa Shrine === A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
=== Cultural Notes === The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
=== Access and Nearby Features === Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == __NOINDEX__[[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]]

== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == __NOINDEX__[[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]]

== References == __NOINDEX__[[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

[[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Obama City]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[category:Sōja shrines]] [[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Obama City]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:bumping for the second subcategory]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[en:draft:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] to [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Obama City]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:bumping for the second subcategory]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Images]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:draft:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

[[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Obama City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:bumping for the second subcategory]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Images]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:draft:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).

* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.

revision.text:
[[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Obama City]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:bumping for the second subcategory]] [[Category:bump-01-18]]
[[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[en:draft:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.

[[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]] [[en:draft:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

revision.text:
[[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Obama City]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sō Shrine (Obama)-qq]] [[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:bumping for the second subcategory]] [[Category:bump-01-18]]
[[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]] [[en:draft:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "q---qq" to "qqqqqqqqq"
revision.text:
[[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Obama City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:tier 4]][[Category: Completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Sō Shrine (Obama)-qq]] [[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:bumping for the second subcategory]] [[Category:bump-01-18]]
[[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[ja:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]] [[en:draft:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->

----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.

* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.

Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (菅原道真) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}}

[[Category:Sōja shrines]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Obama City]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:Tier 4]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]] [[Category: Completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] <!--Sō Shrine (Obama)-->[[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:bumping for the second subcategory]] [[Category:bump-01-18]]
[[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]][[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sō Shrine (Obama)]][[Category:qq]] [[en:draft:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] [[ja:総神社 (小浜市)]] '''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref><!--apparently I need a much longerr page content to get these pages out of the short page list, lets hope this is enough content to successfully do it-->
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (Sugawara no Michizane) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}} [[Category:New]]

== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References == [[en:Sō Shrine (Obama)]] <references />{{Sōja shrines}} [[Category:New]]

* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.

At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
== References ==

'''Sōja Shrine''' (若狭国総社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Obama,_Fukui|Obama]] City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Historically associated with [[Wakasa Province]], it is thought to have served as the provincial shrine (総社) for the region.<ref name=":0">https://genbu.net/data/wakasa/soujya_title.htm</ref>
----
== Deities Enshrined == The main deities (御祭神) enshrined at Sōja Shrine are:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Itsameno-no-Kami''' (五十猛神) * '''Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto''' (大山祇命) Additionally, the spirit of '''Sugawara no Michizane''' (Sugawara no Michizane) is venerated as a merged deity (合祀).
----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.
The crest (神紋) used in the shrine's design is the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' (三つ巴). Additionally, a '''Paulownia crest''' (桐紋) appears on the roof, though its specific connection to the shrine is unclear.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Historical Background == The exact date of the shrine’s foundation is unknown. However, its status as the provincial shrine of Wakasa Province is evidenced in historical records:<ref name=":0" />
* '''Bun'ei 2 (1265)''': The shrine appears in the "Wakasa Province Tax Ledger" (若狭国惣田数帳) as "Sōsha-no-Miya" (惣社宮), marking its earliest known mention. At the time, its importance seemed limited, as the shrine's rice fields were less than one chō in size. * '''Enpō 3 (1675)''': The "Wakasa Province Shrine and Temple History" (若州管内社寺由緒記) notes that the shrine had no resident priests or known traditions.
According to shrine records, the site was originally called '''Miwa Shrine''' (彌和神社) before being renamed '''Sōsha Daigongen''' (総社大権現). Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, it was renamed '''Sōjinja''' (総神社) in 1868. The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----

== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.

----
== Location == The shrine is situated approximately 2 kilometers west of Obama Station, in the Fuchū area. It lies in a village surrounded by the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers. The shrine grounds include a gravel parking lot and the Fuchū Community Center.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Architecture and Structure == The shrine complex features several key structures:<ref name=":0" />
* A '''main hall''' (本殿) and '''worship hall''' (拝殿), alongside a structure resembling a dance hall (舞殿). * The worship hall bears a plaque (扁額) inscribed with "Sōshi" (惣祠), and two additional plaques for "Tenman Shrine" (天満神社) and "Yama-no-Kami Shrine" (山神神社), reflecting the merged deities.

The current name, '''Sōja Shrine''' (総社神社), was adopted on February 28, 1946. ----
== Relationship to Miwa Shrine == A shrine named Miwa Shrine is listed as an ancient shrine (式内社) in Wakasa Province records, raising the possibility of a connection with Sōja Shrine.<ref name=":0" /> ----
== Cultural Notes == The shrine includes several notable plaques and architectural features:<ref name=":0" />
* Plaques bearing the names of the merged shrines, '''Tenman Shrine''' and '''Yama-no-Kami Shrine'''. * The use of the '''Mitsu-Tomoe''' crest, as documented in the "Fukui Prefecture Shrine Register" (福井県神社誌).
Images of the shrine’s torii gate, worship hall, and plaques are often displayed to highlight its cultural and historical significance. ----
== Access and Nearby Features == Sōja Shrine is easily accessible by road, with parking available onsite. Its location between the Kitagawa and Tadagawa rivers adds to its scenic and historical appeal.
